item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Patagonia is a proper noun that refers to a specific geographical region, not a biome. It's not comparable to steppe/plains/mountains at all. Maybe people who live in the region could say they were in the Argentinian or the Chilean part, but just because it's not a state division doesn't mean you can't use it as a location descriptor. It's like saying someone who said they were in 'the Sahara' is ignorant for not saying the 'Algerian Sahara' or 'Egyptian Sahara'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just one nitpick. Patagonia is a big, big definition that's not actually used to design a place in space. It may sound confusing, because the Patagonia is really a place, but it's not a name you would use to call a particular location on Earth you are in. Think of it as steppe, if you say you are on the steppe, I'll immediately think you must be in Mongolia or the parts of China that surround it, but a steppe is just a kind of grassland, it designs a place on Earth but it's not precise and you shouldn't use it as the name of a State, or city. You wouldn't expect people to know exactly where you are on Earth if you say "I'm in the prairie" or "I'm in the mountains", without context. What you should have used is something that in a way defines the section of that particular biome you are referring. Eg: "I'm in the Argentinean/Chilean" Patagonia.

As I said, it's a nitpick and mostly just my opinion. I don't really like when people use "Patagonia" as if it was a country. It sounds ignorant to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm surprised nobody responded to this yet for almost 3 years.

No. The entity inhabited Sandra Oaks from day 182 to 184.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this, all I think it really needs is some sort of addendum concerning what, if any, patterns have been observed with the body surfing. Even if it is truly random just seeing the attempt would feel more in line with SCP methodology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this feels like it has potential. The containment procedures and description sound really really bland and not a good first impression, but the interview logs redeem it. But some people may just exit after seeing the first half so I guess it can be fleshed out better?

Encouragement +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is the end implying that they've been awake for 187 days? I'm pretty sure that would end up killing someone.

Aside from that, there just doesn't seem to be much of a pay off here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept, but…it's overall just meh. +1 for the interesting concept, though. I still enjoyed it overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not buying this one, really. There's some pathos with the entity, but the rest of the elements are just… mushy.

- The extensive detail about the email protocol feels like something that people would omit entirely from other SCPs. I'm not saying it's unnecessary, it's just not very interesting.
- The containment feels like an expedient between real options. Being an uncontained phenomenon isn't a problem, I think all of mine technically are), but here… this feels like we're just getting the snapshot of a story, no beginning for the entity, no end when the Foundation contains it out. SCP narratives don't need those things, but the contents of this particular snapshot (forum post, email brouhaha) aren't particularly interesting.
- It's really hard to believe that this entity can jump into someone for a period of one or multiple days and have significant difficulty figuring out their names. I don't mean this will be easy in every situation, but in many places on Earth, people carry ID, have correspondence, or might have their email or Facebook open on the computer they have open. It can figure out email and forums, for chrissakes. It would make sense if it were cognitively different from a human at all (e.g., incapable of learning, literally can't perceive info about itself, etc.), but that's never implied. (The anomaly would probably be more interesting if it were.)
- The crosslink incident has some good emotional impact, but it doesn't make this SCP more interesting. It would be more interesting if you implied that it jumped into 2878 because/ it was interacting with the Foundation, but you didn't. It was just a random occurrence.

So, what you've got is a pretty basic idea for an entity. You've built up an emotional connection, which is good, but the mechanics of the Foundations involvement are ho-hum, and you haven't put any other twist on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're right that there's a problem with the discovery, and I hadn't thought of that before. How do they even know this is an anomaly and not just some delusional person on the internet? Or a fiction writer? I think this article can work, but it's gonna need to go back to the basic idea, back to the drawing board.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree that the ending came a bit out of nowhere - it might have made more sense to commit fully to the character work, as that was the stronger part of the skip.

I found the early going to be very confusing, and I think it could be rewritten more clearly. The Containment Procedures (not "Special" - is that deliberate?) felt imprecise - in particular the phrase "personnel tasked with containment", as those people don't really contain so much as read emails.

"incorporeal entity of consciousness" would be more meaningful as "incorporeal consciousness" or "non-localised consciousness", I think. "periods of unconsciousness between the current and target host" is also unclear - you mean when they're both asleep, I presume.

The discovery paragraph is quite difficult to believe. Why would the Foundation have noted the mental health forum posts as suggesting any anomalous activity - that level of detailed surveillance breaks my suspension of disbelief. And the idea of reports of those types of behaviours (a) being made in a way (across the entire human population) that is able to be tracked by the Foundation at all; and (b) being sufficiently consistent that the Foundation could determine that they were related - likewise, I don't buy it.

Finally, you have two paragraphs dealing with the set-up of the email address (one before the forum excerpt and one after). The second one is somewhat easier to understand - you'd be better off deleting the first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I still do like this one. It really makes you feel for the dude, especially because he/she can only delay the next jump by not sleeping. It sounds like a hellish existence.

I can get passed the lack of actually containment procedures, primarily because of the online interactions between SCP-2644 and the agent. Skips like these need something to humanize the ordeal, and that provides it. Maybe it's not as eerie or grand as some would like it, but I appreciate it. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will say that the buildup felt strong, and it definitely felt like it had a purpose.

Said purpose never actually materialised, which is disappointing, because this could've ended on a bang and not a whimper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Author, can I edit the part of the Subject Enrique Sánchez?, just to make it more natural to sound.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with TL333s. I feel like there should be compiled information of the bodies they've inhabited, perhaps forming a pattern, perhaps not, but nonetheless that seems like important information. I'm also curious to know about some of the applications of this; does the previously inhabited persons just suddenly appear back into consciousness after the being leaves them or might they stay unconscious indefinitely?

As far as the overall goes, this is well written and has very good dialogue. I'm obligated to +1 but just know that I believe it could be even better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's some interesting stuff in here, like the problems posed by being bilingual but being able to appear anywhere in the world. I'm not sure how I feel about how uncontained this is, though. I mean, you'd think the Foundation could come up with some kind of pattern to what's going on, and just leave the chat logs as a record of how they were able to establish containment or something. Good work with the Agent's communication, though, that felt very realistic. I just sort of feel like this should be Keter, and/or go in a slightly different direction at the end. I mean, "I've been here before" and a crosslink (that doesn't actually work, I should note) isn't a very strong ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Disclaimer: The following skip was inspired from an actual mental health forum post. However, all online records of this forum thread have been deleted. Any person with knowledge or recollection of the phenomena and records of events corresponding with the story of this skip should contact the author.

But seriously, if you remember this too, I'd like to talk to you.
